back: Two vectors u and v in Rⁿ are orthogonal if their dot product u·v = 0. Geometrically, they meet at a right angle (90°).
A set of nonzero vectors {v₁, v₂, ..., vₖ} in Rⁿ is orthogonal if every pair of distinct vectors is orthogonal, i.e., vᵢ·vⱼ = 0 for i ≠ j.
A set of vectors is orthonormal if it is orthogonal and each vector has unit length (norm = 1). For vectors uᵢ, uⱼ: uᵢ·uⱼ = 0 when i≠j, and ||uᵢ|| = 1.
An orthogonal basis is a basis for a subspace where all basis vectors are mutually orthogonal (pairwise perpendicular). Each basis vector is orthogonal to every other basis vector.
An orthonormal basis is an orthogonal basis where each basis vector has unit length (norm 1). It is both orthogonal and normalized.
Given linearly independent vectors, the Gram-Schmidt process produces orthogonal/orthonormal vectors by: v₁ = u₁, v₂ = u₂ - proj_{v₁}(u₂), v₃ = u₃ - proj_{v₁}(u₃) - proj_{v₂}(u₃), etc.
A square matrix Q is orthogonal if Q^TQ = QQ^T = I (identity matrix). This means Q⁻¹ = Q^T. Columns (and rows) of an orthogonal matrix are orthonormal vectors.
A unit vector is a vector with magnitude (norm) equal to 1. To normalize a vector v, compute v/||v||.

front: Define projection of u onto v
back: The projection of u onto v is: proj_v(u) = (u·v / v·v) v. This gives the component of u in the direction of v.

front: What is the orthogonal complement of a subspace?
back: The orthogonal complement W⊥ of a subspace W in Rⁿ is the set of all vectors in Rⁿ that are orthogonal to every vector in W.

front: Why are orthogonal bases preferred computationally?
back: With orthogonal bases, coordinates of any vector can be found easily using dot products: if {v₁, v₂} is orthogonal, then x = (x·v₁/v₁·v₁)v₁ + (x·v₂/v₂·v₂)v₂.
An orthonormal matrix is a square matrix with orthonormal columns. It is also called an orthogonal matrix and satisfies Q^TQ = I.
If W is a subspace of Rⁿ and {v₁, ..., vₖ} is an orthogonal basis for W, then every y in Rⁿ can be written as y = ŷ + z, where ŷ is in W and z is in W⊥.

front: How do you find coordinates relative to an orthonormal basis?
back: If {u₁, u₂, ..., uₙ} is an orthonormal basis, the coordinates of x are simply the dot products: x = (x·u₁)u₁ + (x·u₂)u₂ + ... + (x·uₙ)uₙ.
QR decomposition factors a matrix A as A = QR, where Q has orthonormal columns and R is upper triangular. Q is obtained via Gram-Schmidt process.
